reviews.text: friendship informs identity which informs friendship. it's cyclical—gaining a friend feels like gaining a piece of yourself you didn't know you were missing, and losing a friend feels like losing that piece. over the course of 14 years, close friends Suzanne and Pomme feel separated from themselves when separated from each other. i kept being reminded of this quote from Elaine May'sMikey and Nicky: "I think that's the reason we're such good friends. Because we remember each other from…

reviews.text: Friendships that run deep, that stand the test of time and distance, are beautiful and important. That support and love that Pauline and Suzanne have for each other is real in way that resonated with me. They go through life experiencing hardships and tragedies, but they always have each other to get through it even when they’re apart. When one hurts, the other is there to be stability and comfort. I have a lot of long distance friendships. It’s both…

reviews.text: Cinema as reconstruction, deconstruction and as past and present. Can cinema heal or does it hinder? In returning to the past do we dredge it up or can we make peace with it? The brilliance of this film is in how it leaves some questions open but gives answers to others. To watch this film is to see an argument unfurling real time, it is a step removed from dialectical filmmaking as positions and perspectives shift. Truth and falsity are…
reviews.author: Stephen Gillespie

reviews.text: Incredible. One of the most deceptively complex films I've ever seen, with a setup that seems startlingly simple and straightforward but slowly reveals layers upon layers of ideas and emotions. What struck me the most was how these two men attempted to reconstruct memories from their past, perhaps as a way of exorcising these difficult feelings that they've had for years but also raising feelings of regret and longing for the idealism that they strived for in their younger days.…
reviews.author: Elyes

reviews.text: "Everyone knows best how to direct one’s younger self"That line says everything. It shows how much regret director carries about his past. You can feel he wishes he could go back, guide his younger self, and maybe stop him from making that one violent mistake. Since the film is semi-biographical, it feels like he’s using cinema as a way to talk to his past self, to make sense of who he was, and to find some kind of peace…

reviews.text: Sorta based on a true story on the life of its director Mohsen Makhmalbaf, it tells the rather "unique" coming-of-age story about a teen who stabbed a policeman in a protest rally, what draw him to do that and the aftermath. Performances are really good, but what definitely stands out has to be the heavy influences the film borrows from other filmmakers from the region, especially Abbas Kiarostami when in the end the movie becomes very self-aware, meta and breaking…
reviews.author: Rafael "Mister Movie" Jovine

reviews.text: What a brilliant hall of mirrors, reflecting, distorting and obscuring the truth with its tricky perspectives and playfulness. The director, Mohsen Makhmalbaf, was a revolutionary seventeen year old when he stabbed a policeman while attempting to steal his gun. He ended up serving six years in jail for his crime. Twenty years after the stabbing, Makhmalbaf teamed up with the man he stabbed to make this film. That alone is sensational, but what is really special is the two men’s…

reviews.text: I'm not saying that you must live. but i am asking you not to die. Much of our lives can hinge on a single event or moment. Almost like a divine deity, it arrives unannounced, and forces you to submit to its dictation of your own story. Like after being pushed into a stream, you can thrash about for a while, but eventually have to submit to the unforgiving current. The technical term would be trauma, mostly trauma involving Death…
reviews.author: Lacrimoxa_

reviews.text: Sepia tinted bleakness transforming into a healing process for the ones who stared death in the eye and got to live. "Was escaping death a curse?", is a question that lingers in their psyche; the siblings too numb to verbalise their trauma, and the bus driver trying his best to help himself and them to move past it. These three survivors form a more powerful connection with each other than they had with anyone else, for nothing forms stronger bonds…

reviews.text: 99% Its a meditation on trauma. We never see the bus jacking. We don’t know the mans motives for doing such a heinous crime. We only see it’s devastating psychological impacts on 3 damaged survivors. This is not a story about victims, this is the story of moving on. This is a story of recovery. This is the story of human triumph over darkness. As equally bleak as it is hopeful, it’s generally a tragedy but it never feels overtly…

This film, being the classic that it is, has been picked to pieces, so I'll just leave my personal feelings on the flick here. I had studied this period in my history classes in college, but I think this film depicts the stark desperation of the circumstances better than any text I read in school. I can see why this one…

reviews.text: When it comes to book adaptations, one of the biggest complains these types of project usually get is how they are uncapable of capturing the book. Some do a great job, and they get to catch the essence, but you still feel you're misisng lots of information. Now, I haven't read the original book, so I am talking out my arse here, but right from the first shot I couldn't help but feeling I was transported into a whole new…
reviews.author: Rafael "Mister Movie" Jovine

reviews.text: It's a testament to the power of this story that even a neutered and sanitized version won Best Picture at the Academy Awards in 1940. It's not my favorite film of the year (Pinocchio) but it's certainly the most politically relevant, which is how you win Best Picture Oscars. The film is powerful and polished, but ultimately restricted by the politics of the era. Steinbeck was unflinching, and his novels ended with bleak and intense moments of humanity. This ends…

reviews.text: Cinematic Time Capsule1940 Marathon - Film #43 ”Sure don’t look none too prosperous” The Joads travel from one situation to the next looking for an ounce of hope, and every time it feels like things are finally looking up, this film hits you upside the face like a lead pipe and reminds you that it’s all about despair. This film is the very definition of despair. Every time I watch it I get equal parts depressed and equal parts…

It also has a funny historical anecdote to it: the Soviet Union banned the film because it inaccurately portrayed the farmers as owning cars, according to the censorship. Man, the Soviet Union was a strange country.

reviews.text: "We've sure taken a beatin'" Drought wipes out crop yields in Oklahoma. Banks begin seizing the farms of the families that can't pay their bills. A freshly paroled Tom Joad (Henry Fonda) returns home in the last moments that his family can call the place they lived for so many years by that name. With no other options, they pack up and begin an arduous odyssey to California where the mirage of prosperity waits patiently to be dispelled upon their…

reviews.text: This (and The Emigrants) make up some of the best six-and-a-half hours of my life. I’ve never seen such a beautiful, epic, sweeping, quiet, unforgiving, challenging, terrific, fantastic, truly amazing story. This is the kind of movie that I can’t wait to spend years dissecting. I know I’m being vague, but it’s hard to sum up six-and-a-half hours in a few thoughts. I’m so glad I went on this journey and I can’t wait to do it again.

reviews.text: *Twitch RedeembySeventh_Persona.* On my search of 100/100s. "THE SETTLERS" Now the neighbor's green pasture has dried while stepping on it, but not because of a fire, or because of nature's seasonal behavior: it will be drenched in blood. The idealized "Promised Land" proves to be agolden cagewhere the test of survival has now acquired a different taste: the homesickness has been maximized, the regrets begin to rise to the surface, the savagery now heads on. The…

reviews.text: Swedish Protestant foreplay is talking about how you could die if you have sex. You cross an ocean and brave an unknown world to escape the Christian fascists of your homeland, only to have them follow you and move in next door. Everyone comes to America to build something new, but they all just end up making it in the image of the very things they were trying to escape in first place. That's only a small part of this…

reviews.text: Talk about a Revisionist Western: what if someone made a 3+ hour movie about those sod-buster immigrants who usually only rate a passing scene when they're burned out of their cabins by desperados or the hired guns of cattle barons, or just a shot when found slaughtered by Indians? Also, as an added bonus you get as a subplot a delirious Revisionist take on the Gold Rush myth. [Also, I suppose this film is set in that romantic time that…
reviews.author: Channing Pomeroy

reviews.text: 1972 In Review - February#1 A Swedish immigrant family struggle to establish a new life for themselves in the forest of Minnesota in the mid 19th Century. Following on from the 1971 film The Emigrants,  this picks up straight where that one finished. With our Swedish family now landing in Minnesota we see them making a new life for themselves starting from scratch.  This is an improvement in almost every way over The Emigrants, which was a really good…
reviews.author: Michael501 📺

reviews.text: Quite simply, this is the best film about the immigrant experience -- to America or any other land -- that I've seen. It's a straight sequel to "The Emigrants" from a year earlier, about a group of Swedes and their long and tortuous journey to Minnesota. But whereas I found that Best Picture nominee solid yet predictable and relentlessly grim, this effort made much more of an impression. There's grimness a-plenty here as well. The movie is also quite long…
reviews.author: Todd Hill

reviews.text: Like The Emigrants, it improved in the second half. I was very intrigued in how it took on Native Americans and was pleased we got to see Robert's journey to California, but otherwise I had similar issues as I had for the first. Way too much religious stuff.

reviews.text: 100 Pierce the veil. Assimilation is a threat to the binding ties of community, while music is a precious, transcendent commodity. This is a film about fighting for what is yours. Keeping it close and keeping it safe and being able to share it with the people you love. A siege horror period drama musical. Drips off the screen with sex and style and warm blood. Generational. More thoughts inpodcastform. 🧛‍♂️ 🎶

reviews.text: WOW - absolutely loved this. Terrific performances, some truly jaw-dropping music sequences (that first one at the juke joint is an all-timer), and the vampires are just a metaphorical wrapper for a story about racial and cultural identity, appropriation, and legacy. I thought the film was very, very good as the credits started to roll, but the mid-credit sequence elevated it to absolute greatness for me, and fully completed the emotional arc of the whole movie. Chopra has taken a very cheesy and predictable drama that shows us the journey of an underdog. Yet, he succeeds…

This film serves as a beacon of optimism, dreams, positivity throughout. The presentation of the effort or the whole journey of Manoj Kumar Sharma felt…

reviews.text: “Restart!” There are films which genuinelytryto inspire us. And there are films which actuallydoit. Here’s the latest which belongs to this latter bunch. I hate it when others see me teary-eyed in public. But that climax totally wrecked me. I cried. Vikrant Massey, Vidhu Vinod Chopra, and the entire team, take a bow. You all have given us one of the best films of the year. Stop whatever you’re doing and go watch this film in theatre itself. It’s a must-see!

reviews.text: Cold War dick swinging got the United States government to pour all of its money and resources into the Space Race and we put a man on the moon in eight years. Meanwhile, it also got the Soviet government to pour all of its money and resources into a movie and there will never be another one like it. So enormous and grand and ballsy and beautiful and measured and reckless. Landing on the moon is cool and all but have you ever tried CINEMA!?

reviews.author: sxmran

reviews.text: I can't really describe this; heavens open when two brothers compete like in a legend but the reality is much more unforgiving. It's bitter to see the role of cinema in all of this. It has all the potential we need but we are afraid to trust it. We rush after the change as if it could happen right now and create hierarchies for art to rule the important out of the unimportant but this in itself is political act…
reviews.author: V. Lepistö

reviews.text: I don't even know where to begin. This film was so worth the rewatch because I realized there was so much I didn't grasp before.I remember being a kid and learning about how so many politicians started out as actors, and it was such a foreign concept to me. In a way, it still kind of is, but I think the way this movie brings art and politics together has helped me understand it more. The performances are to…

reviews.author: Chill Kar Sahil

reviews.text: That scene in the middle where Prakash Jha and Tabu lie on the floor and the camera spins above them as he orates poetry. That is my cinematic awakening.
reviews.author: Saahil Thakkar

reviews.text: Every moment I spent with you is unforgettable, even on my deathbed, darling.Cinema, ambition, friendship, politics, intimacy, and power collide masterfully here, whether you call itMani Ratnam’s vision, Rahman’s music, or Santosh Sivan’s lens, each complements the other perfectly. The chemistry between Prakash Raj and Mohanlal surpasses that of any hero heroine pairing, while Aishwarya marks a stunning debut.
reviews.author: Ryan
